name: Kimball Valley
short_description: BLM public land in Kane County offering free rockhounding for agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. No permit required - collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use.
description:
Kimball Valley sits on Bureau of Land Management property in southwestern Utah, offering rockhounds free access to collect semi-precious stones without permits or fees. The valley's colorful geology produces quality agate nodules, chalcedony specimens, and jasper fragments scattered across washes and exposed benches. This remote location attracts collectors seeking quiet, uncrowded rockhounding away from commercial fee dig sites.

The area lies within Utah's Basin and Range province, where Tertiary-age volcanic and sedimentary formations deposited silica-rich minerals over millions of years. Groundwater percolating through fractured rock created the nodules and geodes you find today, with agate and chalcedony concentrated in former lakebeds and wash channels. The exposed geology here makes it an excellent location to study mineral formation in a natural, working landscape.

Expect to walk washes and scan hillsides for weathered specimens. Most productive collecting happens where recent erosion has exposed fresh material - look for color variations in the soil that hint at buried stones. The terrain is open and relatively accessible, though afternoon heat and exposure are factors during summer months. Bring sun protection, plenty of water, and sturdy shoes suitable for rocky ground.

Kimball Valley offers the freedom and low-impact experience BLM rockhounding provides: no crowds, no digging fees, no time limits. You keep everything you collect within the 25 lb daily limit. This is ideal for rockhounders who value exploration and discovery over high-volume collecting.
history: Kimball Valley's agate and chalcedony deposits formed during the Tertiary period when volcanic activity and silica-rich hydrothermal fluids created nodule-bearing formations across the Basin and Range. The valley's current topography, shaped by millions of years of erosion, continuously exhumes these mineral-rich stones into accessible locations for modern collectors.
tips: Focus your search in washes and along eroded hillsides where recent runoff has exposed fresh material - agates here often show banding and color variation. Bring a 10x hand lens to examine specimens for quality chalcedony striping and agate eyes before collecting. Work the area in early morning or late afternoon to avoid midday heat exposure on this open terrain. Look for soil discoloration and small stone fragments as signs of larger nodules eroding nearby.
terrain_notes: Kimball Valley is open, high-desert terrain at approximately 5,400 feet elevation with sparse vegetation consisting of desert shrub and scattered junipers. The landscape is a mix of exposed benches, shallow washes, and rolling hillsides with mostly stable footing. Walking distances from parking areas are typically short to moderate (under 2 miles). The ground is rocky with areas of soft sand in wash bottoms - sturdy hiking boots are essential.
best_season: October - April. The high-desert location experiences extreme summer heat exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit. Fall and winter months provide comfortable collecting temperatures and occasional light rainfall that exposes fresh specimens in washes.
written_directions: From Kanab, drive north on US 89 for approximately 30 miles toward Paria. Turn east onto a marked BLM access road (specific road number varies by season - check with Kane County BLM office for current access). Follow the dirt road for 8-12 miles, bearing southeast. The valley opens on your right. Several small pullouts exist along the access route; use any safe, level spot to park away from the road. High-clearance vehicles recommended for rough sections after rainfall.
road_conditions: Dirt road with washboard surface and potential washouts after rain. High-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle recommended. Road condition varies seasonally - contact Kane County BLM office before traveling.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and flat areas along the access road serve as parking. No developed lot. Space for 5-10 vehicles at any given pullout. Park safely off the road.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Hand tools and small picks are acceptable. Collect loose material and weathered stones only - do not break rock in place. Leave no trash.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or sale
No motorized excavation or power tools
Hand tools and picks permitted
Do not break rock in place
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Kanab, Utah - 30 miles north on US 89. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water available in town.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with rocky footing and uneven washes. No wheelchair access. Steep hillsides in some areas. Loose rock and sharp material present. Not suitable for limited mobility. Good fitness level and sturdy hiking boots required. Terrain is exposed with minimal shade - sun protection essential.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding in Kimball Valley, Utah?
faq.answer: Kimball Valley is known for quality agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. Agates here often display attractive banding and color variation. Most stones are found as weathered nodules and fragments in washes and along eroded hillsides. The chalcedony is particularly sought after by collectors for its translucent properties.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Kimball Valley BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting on this BLM property in Kane County. Personal use rockhounding is allowed under standard FLPMA guidelines. You simply need to stay within the 25 lb per day collection limit and follow basic rules - no motorized excavation, no commercial collection, and pack out all trash.

faq.question: How far is Kimball Valley from Kanab, Utah, and what's the drive like?
faq.answer: Kimball Valley is approximately 30 miles north of Kanab. The drive takes about 45 minutes to an hour from town. You'll travel north on US 89, then turn onto a marked BLM access road heading east into the valley. The final road is unpaved dirt with washboard surface, so all-wheel-drive or high-clearance vehicles are recommended, especially after rain.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for agate at Kimball Valley?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for collecting at Kimball Valley. Summer temperatures exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit on this exposed high-desert terrain, making collecting uncomfortable and risky. Fall and winter months provide pleasant 50-70 degree temperatures and occasional rainfall that exposes fresh specimens in washes.

faq.question: Is Kimball Valley rockhounding good for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Kimball Valley is beginner and family-friendly. The terrain is relatively open, access is free with no permit hassle, and you can collect colorful, quality stones without special equipment. Children ages 8 and up can participate comfortably. The main challenges are the remote location, midday heat during warm months, and the rocky terrain requiring sturdy hiking boots.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Kimball Valley?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand pick or geology hammer for specimen work, a 10x hand lens for inspecting agate banding and quality, a sturdy backpack or collection bag, plenty of water (at least 2 liters), sun protection including hat and sunscreen, and sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain. A GPS device or map is helpful given the remote location. Avoid heavy power tools - hand collecting works best here.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Kanab for agate, chalcedony, and jasper. No permit required. Collect up to 25 lbs daily. Explore Utah's desert geology.
